comments.text: There is recent work in the direction of this problem at Croot, Ernie; Mousavi, Hamed; Schmidt, Maxie, On a conjecture of Graham on the p-divisibility of central binomial coefficients Mathematika 70, No. 3, Article ID e12249, 29 p. (2024). They construct $n$ for which $\binom{2n}{n}$ is divisible by an unusually small number of powers of $p$ for any fixed set of small primes $p$ (e.g., $p=3,5,7$), although they cannot get this number down to zero (which is what is needed for this problem). EDIT: I see now that the already cited paper [BlCr2025] mentions this previous work and improves upon it.

Yes, apologies I should have also cited the Croot, Mousavi, and Schmidt paper (which my paper with Croot improves). For reference, here is what the two papers say in terms of this problem. Given a finite set of primes $P=\{p_1<\cdots<p_k\}$ let $f_P(n)$ be the $P$-divisible component of $\binom{2n}{n}$ (that is, the product of all maximal powers of $p_i\in P$ which divide $\binom{2n}{n}$). It is trivial that $f_P(n) \leq n^{O(k)}$. Croot, Mousavi, and Schmidt proved that, for any $\epsilon>0$, if $p_1$ is sufficiently large depending on $\epsilon$ and $k$, then there are infinitely many $n$ such that $f_P(n)\leq n^\epsilon$. Croot and I in [BlCr25] proved that, provided $p_1$ is sufficiently large depending only on $k$ (e.g. $\geq 10^{94}$ for $k=3$) then, for any $\epsilon>0$, there are infinitely many $n$ such that $f_P(n) \leq n^\epsilon$. So essentially we swapped the quantifiers round so that $\epsilon$ is now independent of the set of primes. We believe that a more careful version of our proof should allow one to prove that, if $p_1$ is large enough in terms of $k$, then there are infinitely many $n$ such that $f_p(n) \leq \exp(O(\sqrt{\log n}))$. (Of course the conjecture is that, generalising the problem of Graham mentioned here, that if $p_1$ is sufficiently large in terms of $k$ then $f_P(n)=1$ for infinitely many $n$.)

Graham has offered a 1000 dollars prize for this problem. This fact has been discussed in Section 4: When is $\binom{2n}{n}$ relatively prime to 105? of this paper by Carl Pomerance. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
